segments.s723-g1: وزان پس is a compact, somewhat archaic sequence meaning 'and from that time / thereafter' (وزان = 'from that time', پس = 'then'). The adjectives جوان و خردمند precede the noun زن here (adjective + و + adjective + noun), a common poetic order equivalent to 'a young and wise woman.'
segments.s723-g2: به آرام here uses به + noun as an adverbial phrase 'calmly / in calm'; بنشست is the past (narrative) 3rd-person singular 'sat down.' با رای زن = 'with the counsel/opinion of the woman' (با + noun phrase); note classical/poetic omission of the ezāfe that in modern prose would be رایِ زن.
segments.s723-g3: چنین گفت is a formulaic 'thus said' (گفت functioning as the past-tense verb 'said'); کامد is an archaic/palatal variant of آمد/آمده 'came/arrived' placed after گفت for poetic word order, so the clause reads like 'thus (it) was said; a new speech came.' یکی نو سخن = 'a certain/new speech' with the adjective نو before سخن.
segments.s723-g4: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that / so that.' جاوید is an attributive adjective 'eternal'; بر دل نگردد is a negative verbal phrase where نگردد (classical present/subjunctive/optative form of گشت/گردیدن) means 'does not become / may not turn' and takes بر دل 'upon the heart' as its locus; کهن 'old/ancient' is the predicate adjective — overall: 'that it may not become old upon the heart.'
segments.s723-g5: جهاندار خاقان is a compounded titulary noun phrase 'the sovereign khaghan.' بیاراستست is a poetic/archaic third-person predicate (here attributing a state to the khaghan) — the verb form indicates a completed or present-state reading 'was/is adorned/arrayed' in narrative diction.
segments.s723-g6: سخنها is the plural subject 'the speeches.' ز هر گونه is a prepositional phrase 'in/from every kind/manner' (z used poetically for 'in/from'), and پیراستست (poetic verb form) predicates 'are adorned/are made ready' — together: 'the speeches were adorned in every manner.' Note the verb follows the noun phrase, typical of Persian word order.
segments.s723-g7: ازو نیست آهو is a negative existential-like structure 'there is not (a) gazelle of/from him' — idiomatically 'he is not a gazelle' (i.e., not merely graceful). بزرگست شاه places the predicate بزرگست ('is great') before its noun شاه — a common inverted order in poetry: 'the king is great.'
segments.s723-g8: دلیر و خداوند توران سپاه strings epithets after the noun: 'brave and lord of Turan's army.' خداوند here is an honorific 'lord/master' governing the following noun phrase توران سپاه ('Turan's host/army'); classical Persian often omits an explicit ezāfe, so syntactically this reads as 'the brave and lord of Turan's army.'
segments.s723-g49: همی‌رفت + خواهم: an archaic/prosodic combination where همی (progressive particle) with the past-stem رفت gives a continuous/habitual sense and خواهم (here modal/future) marks intention — together meaning “I will keep going.” چو تیره شود is a temporal subordinate clause: چو = “when,” تیره = “dark,” and شود (present/future of شدن) = “becomes.”
segments.s723-g50: سر دشمن = “the enemy’s head” (a metonymy for the enemy); از خواب = “from/out of sleep” where از marks the source/cause; خیره شود uses خیره (astonished/staring) + شود (become) to mean “become startled/dazed” or “start out of sleep.” Word order places possessor after noun (سرِ دشمن).
segments.s723-g51: شما = 2nd‑person plural subject. دل به رفتن مدارید تنگ is an admonition: دل تنگ = “narrow/reluctant heart”; به رفتن (literally “toward going”) shows the object of that feeling; مدارید here is a present/subjunctive form addressed to you (an admonitory present), so the whole phrase means “do not make your hearts narrow/reluctant about going.”
segments.s723-g52: که introduces an explanatory/reason clause (“for/because”). از چینیان لشکر آید به جنگ = literal order: از چینیان (from the Chinese) + لشکر (host) + آید (will come) + به جنگ (to battle). آید is present/future sense used to predict arrival.
segments.s723-g53: که begins the clause (“that/for”); خود بی‌گمان is an emphatic adverbial phrase meaning “indeed/undoubtedly”; از پس من = “after me” (از + پس = after/behind); سران (pl.) is the subject “the leaders.” Word order places temporal phrase between the particles and the noun for emphasis.
segments.s723-g54: بیایند is 3rd‑person plural subjunctive/optative used to indicate future/expected action (“they will/shall come”). با + گرزهای گران = instrument/means “with heavy maces,” where گران (adj.) modifies گرزهای (maces, plural).
segments.s723-g55: همه جان یکایک = emphatic plural object “every single life”; به کف = “into the palm” (کف = palm); برنهید is an imperative/command (2nd‑person plural) formed from برنهادن/برنهاد meaning “place upon” — together an exhortation to be willing to give one’s life (“place each life in your hand”). Word order places the object phrase before the verb, typical in Persian imperative constructions.
segments.s723-g56: اگر لشکر آید is a conditional protasis: اگر = “if,” لشکر آید = “the army comes.” دمید و دهید contains two jussive/imperative verbs addressed to the listeners: دمید (here used in the sense “blow” — e.g., a horn) and دهید (2nd‑person plural imperative/subjunctive “give/strike/inflict”), joined by و “and.” The verbs are short, hortatory commands in the battle context.
segments.s723-g33: همه کهترانیم is a nominal clause with the plural present-form/statement کهترانیم ‘we are inferior/lesser’; فارسی places the predicate after the subject phrase. فرمان تو راست is an equational clause: فرمان (your command) is followed by the predicate adjective راست ‘right/true’, with the possessor pronoun تو preceding the adjective phrase.
segments.s723-g34: برین is the fused form of بر + این (= بر این) meaning 'on/with regard to this'; it introduces the following theme. آرزو رای و پیمان is a coordinated noun phrase (wish, counsel and covenant), and تو راست is again an equational predicate applying to them: تو is the possessor of پیمان and راست predicates the whole phrase. The clause is verbless (copular) as is normal with راست.
segments.s723-g35: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' and takes the past verb بشنید 'he heard'. زیشان (ز + ایشان) marks source 'from them'. عرض رابخواند shows the object marker را fused with خواند (رابخواند = را + خواند), so 'he read (recited) the petition.'
segments.s723-g36: درم داد is a simple past clause 'he gave dirhams' with داد the past of دادن. او را contains the accusative/topic marker را attached to the pronoun. به دیوان نشاند is a caused-motion/result clause: نشاند (archaic past of نشاندن/نشستن used transitively) 'he seated/placed' (him) 'in/to the divan' (royal court/seat).
segments.s723-g37: بیامد سپه places the verb before its noun in narrative style ('the host came'). سر به سر is an idiomatic repetitive phrase meaning 'one by one' / 'in turn', and بنگرید is the narrative/look verb 'he looked/examined' (classical narrative form).
segments.s723-g38: This is a numerical + selection clause: هزار و صد و شست is a classical numerical string totaling 1,160 (thousand + hundred + sixty), یل is the noun 'heroes/champions', and برگزید is the past 'he selected/chose'.
segments.s723-g39: کزان here functions as 'from which/of them' introducing a partitive source; هر سواری means 'each horseman', and بهنگام کار is a temporal/adverbial phrase 'at the time of action/when duty calls' with بهنگام governing the noun کار.
segments.s723-g40: نبر is a noun 'battle/campaign' here setting the domain; گاشتندی is a past plural form 'they set/appointed' (classical narrative past). سر از ده سوار is a quantifying expression 'a head over ten riders' i.e. 'one leader for ten mounted men', where سر functions as 'head/chief' and از marks the relation to 'ده سوار'.
segments.s723-g41: درم داد is a noun + past verb construction meaning 'gave a dirham' (درم = coin, داد = 'gave'). The coordinating و + آمد links the next action: آمد سوی خانه باز = 'came/returned toward the house'; سوی خانه is a prepositional phrase 'toward the house' and باز (here) is an adverb meaning 'back/again', placed after the phrase for poetic rhythm.
segments.s723-g42: چنین گفت = 'thus he said' with چنین as an adverb 'thus/so' placed before the verb for emphasis. با لشکر رزمساز attaches as accompaniment: با = 'with', لشکر = 'army/host', رزمساز is a compound adjective/noun meaning 'battle‑making' or 'warrior/war‑ready', describing the host.
segments.s723-g43: که هرکس که ... introduces a relative/conditional clause ('for whoever ...'). The double که is a poetic/colloquial reinforcement of the relative. هرکس = 'whoever'; دید (past) = 'saw/beheld'; او = 'him'; دوال رکیب is a post‑posed descriptive phrase: دوال = 'twofold/pair' (archaic), رکیب here refers to companions/associates (an older usage).
segments.s723-g44: نپیچد is the third person singular negative present/subjunctive form of پیچیدن 'to turn/waver' meaning 'does not turn' or 'would not waver'; in poetry the verb precedes the noun (نپیچد دل = 'the heart does not waver'). اندر فراز و نشیب is a prepositional phrase 'in/among the ups and downs' (فراز = high places, نشیب = low places), giving the sphere in which the heart does not waver.
segments.s723-g45: نترسد is the negative present of ترسیدن 'to fear' (here 'he does not fear'). ز (poetic form of از) = 'from' or 'because of'; انبوه مردم 'a dense/numberless crowd of people' with کشان as a participial/attributive element modifying مردم ('کشان' = 'dragging/pressing', i.e. 'the pressing or sweeping crowd').
segments.s723-g46: گر introduces a conditional 'if/though'. گر از ابر باشد literally 'if (he/they) be from the cloud(s)' with باشد functioning as subjunctive/circumstantial 'should be / were to be'. برو is an imperative 'go' or 'advance'; سرفشان is an epithet/adverbial or vocative whose precise sense is archaic/uncertain here (contextually a descriptive epithet attached to the addressee).
segments.s723-g47: به توران غریبیم = 'we are strangers in Turan' where غریبیم is adjective + 1st‑person plural copula suffix '-یم' ('we are [strangers]'). و بی پشت و یار = 'and without a protector and ally' with بی = 'without' heading a list of nouns; پشت here means 'support/protector' rather than literal 'back'.
segments.s723-g48: میان بزرگان 'among the great ones' is a locative phrase placed before the predicate for emphasis (typical poetic order). چنین سست و خوار = 'so/thus weak and despised' where چنین intensifies the following adjectives سست ('weak/unsteady') and خوار ('lowly/despised').
segments.s723-g25: به نیروی یزدان = an instrumental phrase 'by the power of God'. به + noun expresses the means/instrument; نیروی یزدان is a genitive construction (the power/strength of God). چنو (postposed) = 'thus/such', and بشنود is the third-person present/subjunctive/optative of شنیدن used here in a conditional sense ('should hear' / 'may hear'). Together this segment sets the conditional/if-clause.
segments.s723-g26: بدین چرب گفتار من بگرود = result clause. بدین (ب + این/’by/with this’) links to the previous clause ('by/thereby' or 'thus'); چرب گفتار = 'flattering speech' (چرب = flattering/modulating adjective modifying گفتار); من after گفتار is the possessive 'my' (classical order: گفتارِ من = my speech). بگرود is from گرویدن (بگِرَوَد) meaning 'to turn to/embrace/adopt' and here indicates the person will be won over or adopt that speech. The verbal form is the third-person present/subjunctive/optative used as future/inevitable result in poetry.
segments.s723-g27: بدو گفت هرکس که بانو توی = 'He said to him: Whoever you are, O lady, ...'. بدو گفت is the light verb phrase 'said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto him'); هرکس که introduces a free relative 'whoever/whomever it is who...'; بانو is vocative/predicate 'O lady'; توی here functions as an archaic/poetic second-person copula ('thou art' / 'you are'), completing the clause. Word order and vocative use are typical of older poetic Persian.
segments.s723-g28: به ایران و چین پشت و بازو توی = 'you are the back and the arm of Iran and China.' به + place-names here marks the domain/beneficiary ('for/to Iran and China'); پشت و بازو is a compound metaphor meaning 'the support/back and the arm/strength' (پشت = support/protection, بازو = arm/strength); توی again is the copula 'thou art' linking the predicate to the addressee. The whole segment predicates that the addressee is the support/strength of those lands.
segments.s723-g29: نجنباندت کوه آهن ز جای = 'An iron mountain did not move you from your place.' نجنباندت is a negative past verb (نـ negative prefix + جنباند + ـت enclitic object 'you') meaning 'did not make you move' or 'did not move you'; کوه آهن is the agent/subject 'iron mountain'; ز جای = 'from the place' (ز = 'from' in poetic usage). The verb appears before/with its enclitic object, a common older/poetic order.
segments.s723-g30: یلان را به مردی تویی رهنمای = 'You are the guide of the heroes by your manliness.' یلان را with را topicalizes/emphasizes 'the heroes' (object of guidance); به مردی here means 'by/through manliness' (مردی = manliness/bravery) indicating the quality by which he is leader; تویی is the second-person copula 'you are' and رهنمای is the predicate noun 'guide/leader.' Classical word order puts the marked object before the predicate.
segments.s723-g31: ز مرد خردمند بیدارتر = a comparative phrase 'more alert than a wise man.' ز introduces the standard of comparison ('than'); مرد خردمند = 'a wise man'; بیدارتر is the comparative adjective 'more awake/alert.' This is a terse poetic comparative construction.
segments.s723-g32: ز دستور داننده هشیارتر = the second comparative clause 'than one who knows/understands doctrine.' ز again marks the standard of comparison; دستور داننده = 'one who knows the instruction/order/doctrine' (دستور + داننده 'knower/expert'); هشیارتر is the comparative 'more alert/clear-sighted.' The two halves form parallel comparisons.
segments.s723-g73: برافگند is a past, third‑person singular poetic form (equivalent to برافکند) meaning ‘he threw/cast (upon or off)’. It heads the clause, followed by the noun phrase پر مایه برگستوان, which functions as the direct object: a descriptive compound where پر (feather) + مایه (substance/—‘rich in’) together describe a plumed or feathered mantle, and برگستوان is the location/part associated with it (lit. ‘on the shoulders’ in context). Word order is verb → object, typical of Ferdowsi’s elevated, compact style.
segments.s723-g74: This is a asyndetic (verb‑less) list continuing the previous action: the verb’s result is that he now has / wears the following items. ابا here introduces the list (an archaic/poetic connective whose precise lexical senses given do not match this usage; read functionally as linking or accompaniment). جوشن, تیغ and ترگ + گوان form a coordinated series: ‘a cuirass (جوشن) and a sword (تیغ) and garments like rags (ترگ گوان)’, with گوان (‘like’) post‑modifying ترگ to give a simile. The whole phrase is understood as objects he took on or wore after casting the mantle.
segments.s723-g75: همی‌راند is a continuous/habitual past tense form (همی‑ prefix indicating ongoing action) meaning ‘he was driving/leading’ or ‘he drove on’. The simile چون باد (‘like the wind’) immediately follows to modify the manner of the action. لشکر is the logical subject/object (‘the host/army’) and به راه is a locative phrase ‘along the road’ (به + راه). Word order is verb → simile → subject → locative phrase, a compact poetic ordering.
segments.s723-g76: This is a temporal/qualitative clause modifying the preceding motion: به + adjective+noun pairs give the contrast ‘in/at (times) of …’. رخشنده modifies روز to mean ‘radiant/brilliant by day’; و links to the second coordinate phrase شبان سیاه, ‘(and) black/dark by night’. The construction compresses time and quality: he goes like the wind, bright in daytime and dark at night.
segments.s723-g17: همان is a demonstrative 'that/same' placed before the noun to point back to a previously mentioned person; نیز is a particle 'also/too' following it. پور before a proper name is an epithet meaning 'son (of)' and attaches directly to the name سیاوش. چه کرد is a two-word question ('what did he do?') with چه (interrogative) followed by کرد (past tense of کردن) as the main verb.
segments.s723-g18: ز introduces origin/source ('from') listing two lands joined by و. برآورد is a poetic past/preterite form meaning 'brought forth/mustered/raised' here; گرد is an archaic/poetic element (here part of the verbal phrase) and does not match the simple senses given, so it functions as an auxiliary/poetic marker attached to برآورد.
segments.s723-g19: بسازید is an imperative (plural or respectful) of ساختن 'make/prepare'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that/in order that') and governs the subjunctive that follows. ما is the subject 'we', ز ترکان denotes origin ('from the Turks'), and نهان is an adverb/adjectival complement 'secretly/hidden', modifying how they will depart.
segments.s723-g20: به ایران is the destination ('to/into Iran') with به as the preposition. بریم is the first-person-plural subjunctive/present used after تا to express purpose ('that we may go' / 'let us go'); the provided lemma senses did not include 'to go', so that specific verbal sense is not available in the list. این سخن ناگهان is a short parenthetical phrase 'these words (came) suddenly' with این سخن 'this/these words' and ناگهان 'suddenly'.
segments.s723-g21: به introduces the indirect object/recipient ('to'). گردوی من is the phrase 'my gardūy' (the word گردوی here is literal 'walnut' in the supplied senses but can be a nickname/kenning in poetry); نامه‌ای is the direct object 'a letter', and کرده‌ام is the compound perfect ('I have done/ I have made') forming the present-perfect with the object 'a letter' (i.e. 'I have written/sent a letter').
segments.s723-g22: هم here means 'also/likewise'. از پیش 'before/previously' sets the time frame. تیمار این is the abstract noun phrase 'this care/trouble', with این pointing to something just mentioned, and خورده‌ام is a perfect construction from خوردن used idiomatically to mean 'has befallen me' / 'I have suffered' (not literal 'to eat' here).
segments.s723-g23: که introduces a subordinate/purpose clause ('so that/that'). بر شاه literally 'before/to the king' (بر marking direction/target), پیدا کند is a verbal phrase 'may make apparent / may disclose' with پیدا used as 'apparent/known' and کند the (subjunctive) verb 'may make/do'. کار ما 'our affair/our case' is the object: word order here places the indirect object (بر شاه) before the verb and the direct object (کار ما) before or after the verb as poetic variation.
segments.s723-g24: بگوید is a third-person verb 'may tell/say' (subjunctive/optative), here meaning 'so that he may tell (the king)'. ز before nouns here indicates 'about/of' (tell of our ...). رنج و ز تیمار ما is a coordinated noun phrase 'of our suffering and of our care/trouble' with ز repeated for emphasis; the provided senses for بگوید were noun-centered and did not include the verb sense, so that verbal sense is not present in the supplied list.
segments.s723-g57: وگر is a conditional 'if' (poetic variant of اگر). The phrase بر چنین رویتان نیست رای is a verbless clause with inverted order: literally 'upon such your-faces is-not opinion' → 'you do not hold such an opinion'. رویتان is رو + possessive -تان 'your face(s)/countenance(s)' used figuratively for mind/opinion.
segments.s723-g58: از ایدر مجنبید یک تن زجای is an instruction: از ایدر = 'from here' (poetic order), مجنبید is a negative imperative ('do not move', addressed to plural/you all), یک تن = 'one person' or 'a single man', زجای = 'from the place'. The verb comes before its object phrase, typical of poetic word order.
segments.s723-g59: به آواز is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'in a voice / aloud' (به used adverbially). گفتند is simple past 'they said' (third-person plural). ما کهتریم is a present/stative first-person-plural 'we are lesser' (کهتریم = کهتر + -یم). The clause is reported speech introduced by گفتند.
segments.s723-g60: ز رای و ز فرمان تو نگذریم uses poetic ز (from/of) twice: 'from your counsel and from your command'. نگذریم is first-person-plural present/subjunctive with negative prefix ن- (lit. 'we do not pass/leave'); here it expresses firm refusal/commitment 'we will not abandon'. Word order places the objects before the verb, as is common in Persian.
segments.s723-g61: برین is composed of بر + ین (a poetic/orthographic form of این) meaning 'on/at this' or 'these'. برنهادند is past tense, third-person plural 'they raised/put up' (بر + نهادند). و is 'and'. برخاستند is past plural 'they rose up' or 'stood up'. The sequence describes consecutive actions.
segments.s723-g62: همه جنگ چین را بیاراستند: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject; جنگ چین را is the definite object marker را attaching to 'the Chinese battle/host'; بیاراستند is past plural 'they arrayed/put in order/armed' (bi- + آراستن). The verb takes the whole object phrase and means 'made ready/prepared the Chinese force.'
segments.s723-g63: This is a noun phrase listing participants: یلان 'heroes/warriors' followed by names/appellations sited in apposition (سینه و مهر و ایزد گشسپ). ایزد before گشسپ is an honorific/epithet 'divine' or 'god' attached to the name Goshasp. In Persian, a common noun followed by proper names in apposition lists who belongs to that class.
segments.s723-g64: نشستند is past-tense third-person plural 'they sat' (or 'they took their seats'). با نامداران is an accompanying phrase 'with the renowned/illustrious ones' (نامداران used substantively). بر اسپ uses بر + noun to mean 'upon horses' (on their steeds); Persian often omits a possessive when context supplies it ('on (their) horses').
segments.s723-g9: ولیکن is an adversative conjunction 'but'. چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. با introduces accompaniment ('with') and links the two groups named; ترک and ایرانیان are coordinated as the peoples involved ('the Turks and the Iranians'). Word order (چو + [with‑phrase] + verb) is a common classical pattern.
segments.s723-g10: بکوشد is a 3rd singular classical form of کوششیدن ('to try/strive'); in epic narrative it carries a simple past/historical sense 'he strove'. که here introduces the reason ('because'), not a relative clause. خویشی بود در میان is an inverted copular clause: خویشی 'kinship' is the subject, بود is past 'was', and در میان 'between (them)'.
segments.s723-g11: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'/'out of'. پیوند و بند are two near‑synonymous nouns ('ties/bonds'); وز here functions as a poetic conjunctional element (equivalent to و 'and') linking them — this is an archaic/poetic usage, not the 'wind' sense. آن روزگار = 'that time/age'.
segments.s723-g12: غم و رنج 'sorrow and hardship' are the direct objects. بیند is 3rd singular of دیدن ('to see') used here in narrative with a future/resultive sense ('he sees/will see'). به فرجام کار = 'at/to the outcome of the matter' (به = preposition 'to/at', فرجام 'end/outcome', کار 'affair').
segments.s723-g13: نگر is an imperative 'look!' or 'see!,' calling attention. تا introduces the subordinate clause 'until/so that' here functioning like 'see how/what...' followed by the subject سیاوش and از marking source/agent 'from Afrasiab'.
segments.s723-g14: چه here is an interrogative/exclamatory 'what'. برخورد in this context means 'encounter/what befell' (not physical collision only). جز is the exception particle 'except'. تابش آفتاب is a noun phrase 'the shining/radiance of the sun' (تابش 'radiance', آفتاب 'sun'). The clause asks rhetorically what came of Siawash's meeting with Afrasiab other than the sun's radiance.
segments.s723-g15: سر خویش is a possessed noun phrase 'his own head'; خوی + ش is the root + enclitic possessive (ش = 'his'). داد is simple past 'gave' and here forms the idiom 'سر خویش داد' = 'he gave his head' i.e. 'he lost his life' or 'was killed'. از نخستین بباد = 'from the first blast/gust' (ز/از = 'from', نخستین = 'first', بباد lit. 'to/into the wind' — a poetic way to say 'first onslaught/gust').
segments.s723-g16: جوانی is an appositive or explanatory noun 'a youth'. که introduces a relative clause 'who'. چون او = 'like him'. ز مادر نزاد is a compact past/passive expression meaning 'was born of a mother' (ز = 'from/of', مادر = 'mother', نزاد is an archaic form related to زاد/زادَن 'be born'/'beget'); the clause qualifies the youth by stating ordinary parentage.
segments.s723-g65: همی‌گفت is a habitual/iterative past: literally “kept saying” (the همی- prefix marks ongoing or repeated action in epic narration). هرکس که introduces a free relative (‘whoever, he who’) and is followed by the verbal noun مردن (‘to die’), so مردن به نام functions as a verbal-noun phrase ‘die(d) with a name/reputation’ (به links the noun نام to the verbal noun). Word order is typical classical Persian: the relative phrase + verbal noun precede or integrate into the remainder of the clause.
segments.s723-g66: به از is a classical comparative idiom meaning ‘better than’ (here the copula/infinitive ‘to be’ after زنده is omitted as is common in Persian). زنده = ‘alive’ is the standard adjective; the second conjunct چینیان شادکام is a verbless clause with the copula omitted (i.e. ‘and the Chinese (were) joyful/fortunate’), so شادکام is a predicate adjective describing چینیان.
segments.s723-g67: هم آنگه is a temporal/continuative adverbial phrase ‘then / at once’; سوی کاروان is a prepositional phrase ‘toward the caravan’; برگذشت is a past-tense intransitive verb ‘passed by / went on’ placed at the end (typical SOV order with the verb final).
segments.s723-g68: شترخواست is a compound past: شتر (camel) + خواست (past of خواستن) meaning ‘asked for a camel’. تاپیش او شد should be read as a phrasal sequence ‘تا پیش او شد’ = ‘(one) came up to his front / reached him’ (شد used intransitively as ‘came/arrived’); ز دشت is a locative ‘from the plain’. The verb آمد/شد here is used without an explicit agent for the camel or driver.
segments.s723-g69: گزین کرد is a two-word past tense ‘chose / selected’ (گزین = choose, کرد = did/made). زان اشتران = ز + آن = ‘of those’ (an archaic contracted genitive), so زان اشتران سه هزار = ‘three thousand of those camels’ (poetic inversion places the numeral after the noun-phrase).
segments.s723-g70: بدان here functions as a classical locative/pronominal compound ‘on/to those’ (به + آن → بدان) meaning ‘onto them’. تا in this clause introduces the resulting action, and بنه برنهادند is a verbal phrase ‘they placed/set the bundles (بنه) on (the camels)’ (برنهادند is past plural ‘they put/placed upon’). و بار appends ‘and the loads’ (بار = load/cargo).
segments.s723-g71: چو شب تیره شد is a temporal subordinate clause ‘when/ as night became dark’. گردیه is here a noun (likely a proper name or the assembled party) that functions as the subject of برنشست, and برنشست is past tense ‘sat up’ or ‘got up/took a seat’. The clause sequence places the temporal clause first, then the subject and main verb.
segments.s723-g72: چو here again marks a comparative/temporal sense ‘as/like’; گردی functions as a descriptive noun (here best read as a dialectal/proper-name element or epithet) modified by the adjective سرافراز ‘proud/lofty’. گرزی بدست is a compact phrase ‘with a mace in hand’ where گرزی = ‘mace/club’ and بدست = ‘in the hand’ (the copula is omitted; the phrase describes his posture/appearance).

sentences.s723-s1: From that time the young and wise woman sat down quietly, with the woman's counsel.
sentences.s723-s2: Thus was it said: a new speech arrived, that it should not become old upon the heart.
sentences.s723-s3: The sovereign khaghan was arrayed; the speeches were adorned in every manner.
sentences.s723-s4: There is no gazelle from him; the king is great, brave and lord of Turan's host.
sentences.s723-s25: I will go on; when it grows dark, the enemy will be startled out of his sleep.
sentences.s723-s26: Do not be narrow‑hearted about going, for from the Chinese a host will come to battle.
sentences.s723-s27: For surely, right after me the leaders will come with heavy maces.
sentences.s723-s28: Lay every single life in your palm; if the host comes, blow (your horns) and strike.
sentences.s723-s17: We are all your inferiors, and your command is just; concerning this wish, your counsel and your covenant are right.
sentences.s723-s18: When he heard from them the petition, he read it aloud; he gave dirhams and seated him at the divan.
sentences.s723-s19: The host came and he inspected them one by one; he chose eleven hundred and sixty champions.
sentences.s723-s20: From each horseman, at the time of action, they appointed a leader for every ten riders.
sentences.s723-s21: He gave a dirham and returned toward the house; thus he spoke with his battle‑ready host.
sentences.s723-s22: For whoever saw him with his twofold companions, the heart would not falter amid highs and lows.
sentences.s723-s23: He does not fear the great throng of men; even if (they) come from the clouds, go forth, O sarsfān.
sentences.s723-s24: We are strangers in Turan and without backing or ally; among the great ones we are thus weak and despised.
sentences.s723-s13: If by the power of God he should hear thus, he will be won over to my flattering speech.
sentences.s723-s14: He said to him: 'Whoever you are, O lady, you are the back and the arm of Iran and China.'
sentences.s723-s15: An iron mountain did not move you from your place; you are the guide of the heroes by your manliness.
sentences.s723-s16: More alert than a wise man, more discerning than one learned in doctrine.
sentences.s723-s37: He cast a plumed mantle over his shoulders and took up a cuirass, a sword, and rag‑like garments.
sentences.s723-s38: He drove the host along the road like the wind, brilliant by day and black by night.
sentences.s723-s9: And what did that same son of Siavash do? He mustered (or raised) forces from Turan and Iran.
sentences.s723-s10: Make preparations so that we may secretly slip away from the Turks and go into Iran; these words (were) sudden.
sentences.s723-s11: I have written a letter to my 'gardūy' (literally ‘my walnut’); likewise, previously I have suffered this care.
sentences.s723-s12: so that he may make our case evident before the king and tell (him) of our suffering and our cares.
sentences.s723-s29: And if such counsel is not in your faces (i.e. if you do not hold such an opinion), do not move a single man from here.
sentences.s723-s30: They said aloud in a voice, 'We are the lesser ones; we will not abandon your counsel and your command.'
sentences.s723-s31: They set these up and rose; everyone made ready the Chinese host for battle.
sentences.s723-s32: The heroes — Sineh, Mehr, and the divine Goshasp — sat down, with the renowned men, upon their steeds.
sentences.s723-s5: But when he strove with the Turks and the Iranians — for there was kinship between them.
sentences.s723-s6: From the ties and bonds of that age he saw sorrow and hardship at the outcome of affairs.
sentences.s723-s7: Look — what did Siyâvash encounter from Afrasyâb, save the shining of the sun?
sentences.s723-s8: He gave up his head at the first blast of the wind of youth — a youth who, like him, was born of a mother.
sentences.s723-s33: He kept saying, “Whoever dies with a good name is better than one who lives, and the Chinese are content.”
sentences.s723-s34: Then he went toward the caravan; he asked for a camel, and one came up to him from the plain.
sentences.s723-s35: He chose three thousand of those camels; upon them they set bundles and loads.
sentences.s723-s36: When night grew dark, Gerdīyeh sat up; he sat like a proud warrior, mace in hand.
